elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce a systematic method for constructing gapped domain walls of topologically ordered systems by gauging a lower-dimensional symmetry-protected topological (SPT) order.
elements.sent-2411-002: Based on our construction, we propose a correspondence between 1d SPT phases with a non-invertible $G\times \text{Rep (G)\times G$ symmetry and invertible domain walls in the quantum double associated with the group $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-003: We prove this correspondence when $G$ is Abelian and provide evidence for the general case by studying the quantum double model for $G=S_3$.
elements.sent-2411-004: We also use our method to construct anchoring domain walls, which are novel exotic domain walls in the 3d toric code that transform point-like excitations to semi-loop-like excitations anchored on these domain walls.
elements.sent-2411-005: Most of our understanding of topological order stems from various exactly solvable models.
elements.sent-2411-006: The quantum double~\cite{kitaev2003fault and string-net models~\cite{Levin2005 provided concrete examples to study universal properties of topologically ordered phases; their extensions to three dimensions are also known~\cite{walker20123 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: It is believed that they constitute all gapped phases of matter with a gappable boundary~\cite{kitaev2006anyons,kim2024classifying2dtopologicalphases .}
elements.sent-2411-008: These models can also host a variety of lower-dimensional defects, which can make their physics richer~\cite{Bombin2010,beigi2011quantum,kapustin2011topological,kitaev2012models,Barkeshli2013,Barkeshli2013a,bravyi1998quantum,ostrik2002module,davydov2010modular,davydov2013structure,levin2013protected,kong2014anyon,lan2015gapped,fuchs2014geometric,huston2023composing,xu20242 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The codimension-$1$ defects are called as domain walls, which are the main subject of our work.
elements.sent-2411-010: Domain walls are often understood by folding the system along them and, subsequently, turning the domain walls into gapped boundaries~\cite{kitaev2012models .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In two dimensions, if the underlying anyon model is Abelian, the gapped boundary can be classified in terms of the set of anyons that can be condensed on the boundary, known as the Lagrangian subgroup~\cite{levin2013protected .}
elements.sent-2411-012: More generally, Refs.~\cite{kitaev2012models,kong2014anyon,lan2015gapped,cong2017hamiltonian,kong2022invitation,kong2024higher proposed to use higher category theory to classify the boundaries.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The corresponding mathematical object is a generalization of the Lagrangian subgroup, called the Lagrangian algebra.
elements.sent-2411-014: Beyond studying the condensation of Abelian anyons (0-dimensional topological defects), this framework examines the condensation of non-Abelian anyons and higher-dimensional topological excitations~\cite{kong2024higher .}
elements.sent-2411-015: This approach has been employed in the study of gapped boundaries and domain walls of 2d non-Abelian quantum doubles~\cite{xu20242 as well as 3d topological orders~\cite{zhao2022string,kong2020defects .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, not everything about the domain walls is well-understood, even in two spatial dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-017: For instance, Ref.~\cite{Shi2021 reported a set of superselection sectors and fusion rules at the domain wall whose categorical description is not understood at the moment; domain walls in higher dimensions are even less understood.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In general, in order to study the physical properties of the system it is desirable to have a solvable model whose ground state can be described exactly.
elements.sent-2411-019: The unifying approach to the problem of understanding domain walls that underpins our work is gauging.
elements.sent-2411-020: Recent studies have shown that gauging gives rise to a method of preparing a variety of topological phases~\cite{Tantivasadakarn2024,verresen2022efficientlypreparingschrodingerscat,bravyi2022adaptiveconstantdepthcircuitsmanipulating,Lu2022,Tantivasadakarn2023,Tantivasadakarn2023a,li2023symmetry,li2023measuring,Sukeno2024,lyons2024protocolscreatinganyonsdefects ; gauging has also been explored in the context of quantum error-correcting codes~\cite{Yoshida2016,Vijay2016,Williamson2016,kubica2018,Shirley2019,rakovszky2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The gauging procedure can be implemented using constant-depth adaptive quantum circuits and has been experimentally demonstrated~\cite{iqbal2024non,foss2023experimental,song2024realizationconstantdepthfanoutrealtime,iqbal2024qutrittoriccodeparafermions .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Very recently, such an approach was extended to the more general setting of solvable anyon models~\cite{ren2024efficientpreparationsolvableanyons .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Our work employs a similar approach, but by gauging lower-dimensional defects.
elements.sent-2411-024: For instance, we extensively study the effects of gauging a lower-dimensional symmetry-protected topological (SPT) order embedded in a trivial bulk, symmetric under the same symmetry group.
elements.sent-2411-025: We remark that there are prior works creating defects in a similar way~\cite{barkeshli2023codimension .}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, the way in which we gauge the lower-dimensional SPT is different and leads to the domain walls that, to the best of our knowledge, cannot be obtained by the method from Ref.~\cite{barkeshli2023codimension .}
elements.sent-2411-027: In order to differentiate the two approaches, we refer to the method from Ref.~\cite{barkeshli2023codimension as gauged-SPT domain wall (or more generally, defect).}
elements.sent-2411-028: Our method is referred to as SPT-sewing.
elements.sent-2411-029: The origin of this name will become clear once we explain our method.
elements.sent-2411-030: Although the bulk our work focuses on gauging the standard SPT (based on a group symmetry), we also consider nontrivial SPT phases with a generalized notion of symmetry~\cite{thorngren2019fusion,inamura2021topological .}
elements.sent-2411-031: These generalized symmetries are known as non-invertible symmetries~\cite{mcgreevy2023generalized,schafer2024ictp,shao2023s , and lattice models for various non-invertible SPT phases have been studied~\cite{fechisin2023non,seifnashri2024cluster,jia2024generalized,bhardwaj2024lattice,li2024non,kawagoe2024levinwengaugetheoryentanglement .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: We also discuss such models because there are domain walls that can only be created by gauging an SPT with a non-invertible symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-033: The gauging approach allows us to obtain a variety of domain walls.
elements.sent-2411-034: In fact, in two spatial dimensions, we believe our approach is general enough to construct all gapped domain walls (insofar as the underlying topological order in the bulk can be obtained by gauging some SPT).
elements.sent-2411-035: The main evidence for this conjecture comes from Abelian anyon models, for which we prove that all gapped domain walls can be obtained via our method.
elements.sent-2411-036: The evidence for the non-Abelian case is more scarce; nonetheless, we provide some nontrivial examples, such as the quantum double model for the symmetric group $S_3$.
elements.sent-2411-037: In three spatial dimensions, we obtain conceptually novel domain walls, which we call anchoring domain walls.
elements.sent-2411-038: Anchoring domain walls transform point-like excitations into semi-loop-like excitations anchored on them, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-039: We find that there are two types of such domain walls, which exhibit different physics.
elements.sent-2411-040: Namely, one type hosts an Abelian anyon model at the domain wall whereas the other one hosts a non-Abelian anyon model.
elements.sent-2411-041: To the best of our knowledge, such domain walls have not been studied before.
elements.sent-2411-042: How they fit with the existing categorical classification of lower-dimension defects~\cite{kong2024higher remains unclear.}
elements.sent-2411-043: We simply discuss their constructions and pertinent properties, leaving the classification question for future work.
elements.sent-2411-044: The rest of this article is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-045: In Section~\ref{sec:2d_toric_code , we describe our method with a simple example of the 2d toric code~\cite{kitaev2003fault .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: In Section~\ref{sec:gauging_map , we introduce the convention used throughout our work and review how gauging works.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In Section~\ref{sec:2d_Abelian_general , we construct all possible gapped domain walls for Abelian quantum doubles in two dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-048: In Section~\ref{sec:non-Abelian , we apply our method to the non-Abelian quantum double model for $S_3$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: In Section~\ref{sec:3ddomainwall , we discuss various exotic domain walls in the 3d toric code and some simplified models are presented in Section~\ref{sec:3d_generalized_pauli .}}
elements.sent-2411-050: We end with a discussion in Section~\ref{sec:discussion .}
elements.sent-2411-051: In this section, we explain our method with a simple example of the 2d toric code.
elements.sent-2411-052: First, we briefly review this model and the gauging map we use.
elements.sent-2411-053: We then explain how gauging can be used to construct domain walls of the 2d toric code.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Toric code and the gauging map
elements.sent-2411-054: The toric code~\cite{kitaev2003fault is defined on a square lattice, wherein each edge hosts one qubit.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The Hamiltonian contains commuting four-body interaction terms, often referred to as the vertex ($A_v$) and plaquette ($B_p$) terms \beq H =-\sum_{v\in V A_v -\sum_{p\in F B_p =-\sum_{v\in V \prod_{v\in e X_e -\sum_{p\in F \prod_{e\in p Z_e, \eeq where $V$ and $F$ are the sets of vertices and plaquettes, respectively.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: The ground states are thus the simultaneous $+1$-eigenstates of $A_s$ and $B_p$.
elements.sent-2411-057: If a quantum state $\ket{\Psi}$ is a $-1$-eigenstate of a certain vertex (plaquette) term, we say that $\ket{\Psi}$ has a point-like $e$ ($m$) excitation on that vertex (plaquette).
elements.sent-2411-058: A pair of such excitations can be created by applying a string of Pauli $Z$ or $X$ operators on the ground state.
elements.sent-2411-059: A ground state of the toric code can be obtained via a gauging map applied to the trivial fixed-point state of the $\mathbb{Z _2$ symmetry, i.e., $\ket{+ ^{\otimes V}$~\cite{yoshida2017gapped .}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The gauging map is a bijective map between states with global symmetry $\mathbb{Z _2$ and states with a gauge symmetry $\mathbb{Z _2$ on the same lattice, but with the Hilbert space associated with the vertices for the former and with the edges for the latter.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: It is convenient to describe the action of the gauging map on local operators.
elements.sent-2411-062: The map converts a Pauli $X$ operator on a vertex $v$ to $\prod_{v\in e X_e$.}
elements.sent-2411-063: It also converts a Pauli operator of the form $Z_v Z_{v'}$ to $Z_e$, where $e$ is an edge connecting $v$ and $v'$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Upon gauging, the $\mathbb{Z _2$ global symmetry becomes the $\mathbb{Z _2$ gauge symmetry.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: This implies that the state $\ket{+ ^{\otimes V}$ gets mapped to a ground state of the toric code; see Fig.~\ref{fig:gauging_1 .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{gauging_1.pdf} \caption{Schematic description of the gauging map. Local symmetric operators are mapped to local operators on the right lattice. For instance, $X_v$ is mapped to $\prod_{v\in e}X_e$, and $Z_{v}Z_{v'}$ on adjacent vertices is mapped to $Z_{\langle v,v'\rangle}$ on the edge. As a result, two different boundary Hamiltonians become the smooth and rough boundary.} \label{fig:gauging_1}
elements.sent-2411-066: For the lattice with open boundary conditions, the gauging map yields gapped boundaries of the toric code.
elements.sent-2411-067: Let $\Lambda$ be a square lattice with open boundary conditions and denote its boundary as $\partial\Lambda \subset \Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-068: Different boundaries of the toric code can be obtained by choosing different boundary Hamiltonians with respect to the $\mathbb{Z _2$ global symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-069: There are only two allowed possibilities, the symmetry and spontaneously symmetry breaking phases.
elements.sent-2411-070: After gauging, they become different toric code boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-071: The smooth boundary~\cite{bravyi1998quantum can be obtained from the product state $\ket{+ ^{\otimes \Lambda}$, which can be thought of as the ground state of a $\mathbb{Z _2$ symmetric Hamiltonian \beq H_1 =H_{\text{bulk + H_{\text{boundary =-\sum_{v\in \Lambda \backslash \partial\Lambda X_v - \sum_{v\in \partial\Lambda X_v.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: The rough boundary~\cite{bravyi1998quantum can be obtained by changing the boundary term to a $\mathbb{Z _2$-symmetry breaking term \beq H'_{\text{boundary = - \sum_{\langle v,v'\rangle\in \partial\Lambda Z_v Z_{v' .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: On the smooth (rough) boundary, a single $m$ ($e$) particle can be annihilated.
elements.sent-2411-074: When that happens, we say that $m$ ($e$) is condensed on that boundary~\cite{bombin2008family,beigi2011quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-075: These two boundaries are the only possible boundaries for the 2d toric code~\cite{kapustin2011topological,kitaev2012models,ostrik2002module,davydov2010modular,davydov2013structure,kong2014anyon,lan2015gapped,fuchs2014geometric .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Gapped phases of matter can host a gapped domain wall, which is a codimension-$1$ defect.
elements.sent-2411-077: For the toric code, there are six types of domain walls.
elements.sent-2411-078: Some of these domain walls are invertible, meaning that all the anyons can pass through.
elements.sent-2411-079: There are two invertible domain walls, the trivial one and the one exchanging the $e$ and $m$ particles.
elements.sent-2411-080: We refer to them as $S_1$ and $S_{\psi}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-081: The other domain walls are non-invertible and are labeled by the anyons that can condense on them, i.e., $S_e, S_m, S_{em ,$ and $S_{me}$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The non-invertible domain walls can be obtained via the method in Section~\ref{sec:toric_code_1 .}
elements.sent-2411-083: We thus focus on the construction of invertible domain walls, in particular the $S_{\psi}$ domain wall.
elements.sent-2411-084: There are many different ways of constructing $S_{\psi}$, e.g., via a lattice defect~\cite{Bombin2010,kitaev2012models .}
elements.sent-2411-085: Recently, Ref.~\cite{barkeshli2023codimension showed that $S_{\psi}$ can be constructed from gauging a 1d Kitaev Majorana fermion chain.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Ref.~\cite{roumpedakis2023higher showed from topological field theory that $S_{\psi}$ can be constructed from higher-gauging on a codimension-1 surface in the spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-087: Ref.~\cite{tantivasadakarn2023string showed that the domain walls can be obtained from a toric code ground state by applying a linear-depth unitary sequential circuit.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Here we take a different approach to constructing the $S_{\psi}$ domain wall by considering two disconnected lattices with close-by boundaries; see Eqs.~\eqref{eq:Spsi_main -\eqref{eq:strivial for illustration.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Note that there is a $\mathbb{Z _2$ global symmetry on each side.}
elements.sent-2411-090: We can then obtain an invertible domain wall by decorating a nontrivial $\mathbb{Z _2\times\mathbb{Z _2$ SPT state on the close-by boundaries and gauging.}}

elements.sent-2411-092: By placing the even and odd sites on the left and right close-by boundaries, we obtain an SPT compatible with the global symmetry we imposed.
elements.sent-2411-093: After applying the gauging map to this setup, the stabilizers of the SPT are transformed as follows \begin{equation}\adjincludegraphics[width=0.8\columnwidth,valign=c]{Spsi.pdf}.\label{eq:Spsi_main}\end{equation} This domain wall is invertible because the $e$ and $m$ particles from one side can pass through.

elements.sent-2411-095: Therefore, we realized the $S_{\psi}$ domain wall by gauging a 1d SPT under the $\mathbb{Z _2\times \mathbb{Z _2$ symmetry.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Because there is only one nontrivial 1d SPT under the $\mathbb{Z _2\times \mathbb{Z _2$ symmetry, one cannot hope to sew a $\mathbb{Z _2\times \mathbb{Z _2$ SPT to obtain the transparent domain.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: However, we can instead consider a lattice defect line with three global $\mathbb{Z _2$ symmetries.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The following is a 1d $\mathbb{Z _2\times \mathbb{Z _2\times \mathbb{Z _2$ SPT, which after gauging returns the trivial domain wall \begin{equation}\adjincludegraphics[width=0.8\columnwidth,valign=c]{S1_2.pdf}. \label{eq:strivial}\end{equation} The SPT wavefunctions in Eqs.~\eqref{eq:Spsi_main -\eqref{eq:strivial can be constructed from the topological action~\cite{wang2015field,tsui2020lattice,chen2023higher,li2023measuring ; see Appendix~\ref{appx:topologicalaction for a pedagogical introduction.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-099: For each $\mathbb{Z _2$ symmetry, we introduce a $\mathbb{Z _2$-valued background gauge field, denoted as $A_i, i\in \{1,2,3 \}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Then, the topological action for $S_{\psi}$ is \beq S_{\text{top =\int\frac{1 {2 A_1\cup A_2.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Importantly, this approach for constructing SPT wavefunctions readily generalizes to any Abelian symmetry group.
elements.sent-2411-102: Attentive readers might notice a similarity between SPT-sewing and gauged-SPT defects~\cite{barkeshli2023codimension , since both approaches construct domain walls by gauging a trivially symmetric state decorated by lower-dimensional SPTs.}
elements.sent-2411-103: However, there are important differences between the two, as we now explain.
elements.sent-2411-104: First, for gauged-SPT defects the gauging map is applied throughout the bulk.
elements.sent-2411-105: Consequently, the gauged-SPT defects are always invertible.
elements.sent-2411-106: However, in SPT-sewing the gauging map is applied on two disconnected lattices and the obtained domain walls may or may not be invertible.
elements.sent-2411-107: Second, the gauged-SPT defects arising from bosonic SPTs are always flux-preserving domain walls.
elements.sent-2411-108: On the other hand, the $S_{\psi}$ domain wall in the toric code is not flux-preserving.
elements.sent-2411-109: Thus, one should consider decorating a Kitaev Majorana fermion chain with a $\mathbb{Z _2$ fermion parity symmetry, such that after gauging $S_{\psi}$ is obtained~\cite{barkeshli2023codimension .}}
elements.sent-2411-110: However, in a generic topological order, it is not clear if all the invertible domain walls can be constructed this way.
elements.sent-2411-111: On the other hand, the SPT-sewing constructions of invertible domain walls only use bosonic SPTs, and should be readily generalizable to domain wall constructions in generic topological orders.
elements.sent-2411-112: We now turn to such generalizations.

elements.section-2411-004: Domain walls in the 2d Abelian quantum double
elements.sent-2411-132: In this section, we generalize SPT-sewing from Section~\ref{sec:2d_toric_code to the 2d quantum double model for any Abelian group $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-133: The main result of this section is that all the domain walls can be obtained from $G\times G\times G$ SPT-sewing.
elements.sent-2411-134: To study the general SPT-sewing construction of domain walls, we first review the domain walls in the $G$ quantum double obtained by gauging $G$ symmetric SPT phases.
elements.sent-2411-135: These domain walls are extensively studied in Ref.~\cite{barkeshli2023codimension ; we refer to them as the gauged-SPT domain walls.}
elements.sent-2411-136: Using the gauged-SPT domain walls and the folding trick, we will be able derive the properties of the general SPT-sewn domain walls.
elements.sent-2411-137: Let us first pick a 1d line on the lattice, where there is a natural global $G$ symmetry action.
elements.sent-2411-138: We then put a symmetric product state $\ket{+ ^{\otimes V}$ on the lattice, and decorate the line with a 1d SPT with a $G$ symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-139: The gauging map will take this decorated state into a ground state of the quantum double model, with a gapped domain wall on the line.
elements.sent-2411-140: If we fuse together two such domain walls obtained by gauging SPT$_1$ and SPT$_2$, the resulting domain wall should be equivalent to the one obtained from gauging the stacked SPTs (usually denoted as $\text{SPT _1\boxtimes\text{SPT _2$).}}
elements.sent-2411-141: For all the SPT states under finite symmetry group, stacking certain number of copies of the SPT state results in a trivial SPT, meaning the set of domain walls obtained this way should have a group structure.
elements.sent-2411-142: Therefore, the gauged-SPT domain walls are always invertible, i.e., all the anyons can pass through them.
elements.sent-2411-143: Similarly to the invertible $S_{\psi}$ in the toric code, there could be an anyon exchange after passing through an invertible domain wall.
elements.sent-2411-144: Ref.~\cite{barkeshli2023codimension shows that the gauged-SPT domain walls correspond to some ``flux-preserving'' maps on anyons.}
elements.sent-2411-145: When $G$ is Abelian, any anyon is specified by a pair $(e,m)$, where $e$ is the charge and $m$ is the flux, and this map is given by the slant product of the 2-cocycle\footnote{In general, a 1d SPT state under a $G$ symmetry corresponds to a 2-cocycle representative $\nu$, where $\nu$ is a map from $G\times G$ to $U(1)$ satisfying the cocycle condition.}
elements.sent-2411-147: Every finite Abelian group is isomorphic to a product of cyclic groups, hence $G=\prod_i \mathbb{Z _{n_i}$.}
elements.sent-2411-148: For such an Abelian group, we can understand the 2-cocycle $\nu$ via its corresponding SPT topological action; see Appendix \ref{appx:topologicalaction for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-149: The general form of the topological action is given by a sum over terms that couple between two cyclic subgroups \beq S_{\text{top =\int\sum_{i<j}\frac{k_{i,j {\text{gcd (n_i,n_j) A^{(i)}\cup A^{(j) , \eeq where $A^{(i)}$ is the $\mathbb{Z _{n_i}$-valued background gauge field, and $k_{i,j =0,1,\cdots,\text{gcd (n_i,n_j)-1$.}}}}}}}}}}

elements.section-2411-007: Simple models of the exotic domain walls
elements.sent-2411-459: In this section, we describe simplified models that produce the same physics as the ones described in Sections~\ref{subsec:3d_type-I and~\ref{subsec:3d_type-II .}}
elements.sent-2411-460: There are two advantages of these simplified models.
elements.sent-2411-461: First, they are defined on a cubic lattice, similar to the well-known 3d toric code model.
elements.sent-2411-462: Second, they are (generalized) Pauli models~\cite{ellison2022pauli , which make them easy to study.}
elements.sent-2411-463: We consider a cubic lattice throughout this section.
elements.sent-2411-464: In the bulk, each edge hosts one qubit, and the 3d toric code model is given as in Eq.~\eqref{eq:3d toric code , \beq H =-\sum_{v\in V A_v -\sum_{p\in F B_p, \eeq where $A_v$ is given by \begin{equation}\adjincludegraphics[width=0.25\columnwidth,valign=c]{3dTC_3.pdf},\end{equation} and $B_p$ for different plaquettes are given by \begin{equation}\adjincludegraphics[width=0.75\columnwidth,valign=c]{3dTC_2.pdf}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-465: We now describe a simple model of the type-I domain wall.
elements.sent-2411-466: Away from the domain wall, each edge hosts a single qubit.
elements.sent-2411-467: For the edges on the domain wall, we introduce two qubits per site.
elements.sent-2411-468: These two qubits can be viewed as a four-dimensional qudit.
elements.sent-2411-469: The set of operators acting on this qudit is generated by the generalized Pauli $\widetilde{X}$ and $\widetilde{Z}$ operators \begin{equation}\widetilde{X} = \sum_{h=0}^3| h+1\ mod\ 4\rangle\langle h|, \ \widetilde{Z} = \sum_{h=0}^3 i^{h} |h\rangle\langle h|.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-470: Because the stabilizers away from the domain wall are the same as that of the 3d toric code, we focus on the stabilizers at the domain wall.
elements.sent-2411-471: The domain wall stabilizers are given as follows \begin{equation}\adjincludegraphics[width=\columnwidth,valign=c]{type_I_DW.pdf}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-472: The domain wall contains the plaquette depicted as the second term and its translations in the $x$ and $y$ directions.
elements.sent-2411-473: Here $L$ and $R$ represent the qubits lying to the left and the right of the domain wall.
elements.sent-2411-474: On the domain wall there are four anyons: $1$, $s$, $\bar{s}$ and $s\bar{s}$.
elements.sent-2411-475: The fusion rules are given by \begin{equation}s \times s = 1, \quad \bar{s} \times \bar{s} = 1, \quad s \times \bar{s} = s\bar{s}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-476: The anyon \( s \) is a semion, while \( \bar{s \) is an anti-semion.}
elements.sent-2411-477: On the domain wall, pairs of $s$, $\bar{s}$, and $s \bar{s}$ are created by the following operators\footnote{Here we only consider creating anyon pairs in a specific direction.}
elements.sent-2411-478: For the full discussion we refer the reader to Ref.~\cite{ellison2022pauli . \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} W^s = \adjincludegraphics[width=1.3cm, valign=c]{DS_1.pdf}, W^{\bar{s}} = \adjincludegraphics[width=1.3cm, valign=c]{DS_2.pdf}, W^{s \bar{s}} = \adjincludegraphics[width=0.6cm, valign=c]{DS_3.pdf}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Note that when a pair of $s$ or $\bar{s}$ anyons is created, two semi-loops of magnetic flux are also generated in the bulk on both sides of the domain wall.}

elements.sent-2411-508: Furthermore, the operator in Eq.~\eqref{eq:TQD_5 violates the stabilizer in Eq.~\eqref{eq:gaugedZ4SPT (c).}}

elements.sent-2411-511: In this article, we have introduced SPT-sewing, which is a systematic method of constructing gapped domain walls of topologically ordered systems.
elements.sent-2411-512: Compared to the prior works~\cite{barkeshli2023codimension,ren2024efficientpreparationsolvableanyons , where gauging was used to create lower-dimensional defects from defects in a simpler topologically ordered (or trivial) phase, SPT-sewing uses a lower-dimensional SPT to entangle two disconnected topological orders.}
elements.sent-2411-513: Using SPT-sewing, we proved that all the invertible domain walls in the 2d Abelian $G$ quantum double model can be constructed from $G\times G\times G$ SPT-sewing, and illustrated the discussion with several examples.
elements.sent-2411-514: We conjecture that all the invertible domain walls in a generic 2d quantum double can be constructed from SPT-sewing with a non-invertible $G\times \text{Rep (G)\times G$ symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-515: Our investigation of the $S_3$ quantum double model provides a nontrivial evidence of this conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-516: We also used our method of SPT-sewing to construct anchoring domain walls, which are exotic domain walls in the 3d toric code.
elements.sent-2411-517: We provided the domain wall lattice models constructed by sewing the canonical 2d SPT states on a triangular lattice given by the 3-cocycles, as well as the equivalent but simpler domain wall lattice models on a cubic lattice.
elements.sent-2411-518: These domain walls transform point-like excitations into semi-loop-like excitations anchored on them.
elements.sent-2411-519: As such, anchoring domain walls appear to generalize the $e\leftrightarrow m$ exchange domain wall~\cite{Bombin2010 to higher dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-520: There are several future directions to pursue.
elements.sent-2411-521: First, it is natural to expect that SPT-sewing will lead to efficient methods of creating gapped domain walls in topologically ordered lattice models.
elements.sent-2411-522: Since the gauging map for solvable groups can be implemented via constant-depth adaptive circuits~\cite{verresen2022efficientlypreparingschrodingerscat,Lu2022,bravyi2022adaptiveconstantdepthcircuitsmanipulating,Tantivasadakarn2024 , we anticipate the domain walls for Abelian models, the $C\leftrightarrow F$ domain wall and the exotic domain walls in 3d that we described in our work to be also implementable via constant-depth adaptive circuits.}
elements.sent-2411-523: Second, it is natural to ask whether there is a field-theoretical description of the SPT-sewing construction and the models we discovered.
elements.sent-2411-524: To the best of our knowledge, the anchoring domain walls in the 3d toric code have not been studied before and they appear not to fit into the existing categorical classification of lower-dimensional defects in Ref.~\cite{kong2024higher .\footnote{We thank Liang Kong for the related discussion.}}

elements.sent-2411-569: In this subsection, we provide a brief introduction to the topological action, and its corresponding fixed-point SPT wavefunctions.
elements.sent-2411-570: For a $(d+1)$-dimensional $G$-SPT state, the topological action is given by \begin{equation}S^{top} \left[M, A\right] = 2 \pi i \int_M L[A],\end{equation} in which $A$ is a background gauge field that is a $G$-valued 1-cocycle, and $M$ is the spacetime manifold.
elements.sent-2411-571: The Lagrangian $\mathcal{L [A]$ is a topological invariant and satisfies, \begin{equation}\delta L = 0,\end{equation} where the coboundary operator $\delta$ is defined in Eq.~\eqref{eq:coboundary .}}
elements.sent-2411-572: Two Lagrangians differed by a coboundary $\delta \Theta$ are equivalent, \begin{equation}L' = L + \delta \Theta,\end{equation} where $\Theta$ is a $d$-cochain.
elements.sent-2411-573: Distinct classes of inequivalent Lagrangians give rise to different bosonic $G$-SPT phases, which are classified by the cohomology group $\mathcal{H ^{d+1 (G, U(1))$.}}
elements.sent-2411-574: To obtain the corresponding SPT state, one could write the physical action of the theory in terms of the physical fields (matter fields) from the topological action.

elements.section-2411-014: Domain walls in the 3d toric code: calculation details
elements.sent-2411-842: In this section, we provide calculation details for the domain walls discussed in Section~\ref{sec:3ddomainwall .}
elements.sent-2411-843: In all the cases, we will start from a trivial SPT, apply the SPT entanglers, and then gauge the entire system.
elements.sent-2411-844: We discuss the type-I, II, and III domain walls in Section~\ref{appx:Z2TQD , ~\ref{appx:Z22TQD , and~\ref{appx:Z23TQD .}}}
elements.sent-2411-845: Let us remind the readers of our convention.
elements.sent-2411-846: We shall employ a lattice structure in Fig.~\ref{fig:lattice_structure_3d .}
elements.sent-2411-847: The vertices of this lattice can be partitioned into three sets $R$, $G$, and $B$.
elements.sent-2411-848: Vertices in these sets and the orientation of the edges are described as follows: \begin{equation}\adjincludegraphics[width=0.3\linewidth,valign=c]{branching_3.pdf}.\label{eq:branching_3}\end{equation} With respect to this lattice, the SPT entanglers are constructed using the method of Ref.~\cite{yoshida2015topological,yoshida2017gapped , which amounts to assigning the following phase factor to each triangle: \begin{equation}\adjincludegraphics[width=2.5cm,valign=c]{2simplex_1.pdf} = \omega (g_3, g_3^{-1} g_2, g_2^{-1} g_1), \quad \adjincludegraphics[width=2.5cm,valign=c]{2simplex_2.pdf} = \omega^{-1} (g_3, g_3^{-1} g_2, g_2^{-1} g_1), \label{eq:SPTentangler}\end{equation} where $\omega$ is an appropriate cocycle.}
elements.formula-2411-159: \begin{equation}\adjincludegraphics[width=2.5cm,valign=c]{2simplex_1.pdf} = \omega (g_3, g_3^{-1} g_2, g_2^{-1} g_1), \quad \adjincludegraphics[width=2.5cm,valign=c]{2simplex_2.pdf} = \omega^{-1} (g_3, g_3^{-1} g_2, g_2^{-1} g_1), \label{eq:SPTentangler}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-849: We begin by constructing a nontrivial $\mathbb{Z _2$ SPT on a codimension-1 submanifold.}
elements.sent-2411-850: The SPT on this submanifold is called the type-I SPT, which is equivalent to the Levin-Gu SPT~\cite{levin2012braiding .}
elements.sent-2411-851: The corresponding type-I 3-cocycle is given by~\cite{yoshida2017gapped,propitius1995topological \begin{equation}\omega_I (g_1, g_2, g_3) = \exp\left(\frac{p \pi i}{2} g_1 (g_2 + g_3 - \left[g_2 + g_3\right])\right),\end{equation} where $g_1, g_2, g_3 \in \{0,1\}$ correspond to the states at three vertices of a triangle, and $\left[g_2 + g_3\right] = g_2 + g_3 \mod 2$.}
elements.formula-2411-160: \begin{equation}\omega_I (g_1, g_2, g_3) = \exp\left(\frac{p \pi i}{2} g_1 (g_2 + g_3 - \left[g_2 + g_3\right])\right),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-852: Equivalently, the nontrivial 3-cocycle can be written in the following form \begin{equation}\omega_{I}(g_1, g_2, g_3) = (-1)^{g_1 g_2 g_3}, \label{eq:type_I_entangler}\end{equation} which will be more convenient for us.
elements.sent-2411-853: Letting $\omega=\omega_I$, we obtain the SPT entangler.
elements.sent-2411-854: For the purpose of obtaining the stabilizers of the SPT Hamiltonian, it suffices to obtain the entangler acting nontrivially on a single site, such as the following: \begin{equation}\adjincludegraphics[width=0.25\linewidth,valign=c]{branching_4.pdf}.\label{eq:branching_4}\end{equation} With respect to these sites, we obtain the following SPT entangler (specifically, consisting only of gate acting nontrivially on $g_0$) \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \quad \frac{\omega_I (g_0, g_0^{-1} g_1, g_1^{-1} g_2) \omega_I (g_0, g_0^{-1} g_3, g_3^{-1} g_4) \omega_I (g_0,g_0^{-1} g_5, g_5^{-1} g_6)}{\omega_I (g_0, g_0^{-1} g_3, g_3^{-1} g_2) \omega_I (g_0, g_0^{-1} g_5, g_5^{-1} g_4) \omega_I (g_0, g_0^{-1} g_1, g_1^{-1} g_6)} = \exp\left(\pi i g_0 (g_1 g_2 - g_2 g_3 + g_3 g_4 - g_4 g_5 + g_5 g_6 - g_6 g_1)\right) = \mathrm{CCZ}_{0,1,2} \mathrm{CCZ}_{0,2,3} \mathrm{CCZ}_{0,3,4} \mathrm{CCZ}_{0,4,5} \mathrm{CCZ}_{0,5,6} \mathrm{CCZ}_{0,6,1}, \label{eq:entangler_1} \end{aligned}\end{equation} in which $\mathrm{CCZ _{i, j, k}$ is the $\mathrm{CCZ}$ gate between qubits $i$, $j$, and $k$.}
elements.sent-2411-855: The same result applies to every vertex, independent of their type ($R, G,$ and $B$).
elements.sent-2411-856: Thus the SPT entangler is a product of $\mathrm{CCZ}$ on every plaquette.
elements.sent-2411-857: Therefore, the fixed-point SPT wavefunction is given by the following, \begin{equation}|\psi\rangle = \sum_{\{g_v\}} \prod_{\triangle_{123}} \omega^{s(\triangle_{123})}_{I}(g_3, g_3^{-1} g_2, g_2^{-1} g_1) \bigotimes_v | g_v\rangle,\end{equation} in which $g_v$ is the state at site $v$, $\triangle_{123}$ is a 2-simplex (triangular face), and $s(\triangle_{123 ) = \pm 1$ denotes the orientation of the triangle.}
elements.sent-2411-858: Conjugating the local stabilizers of the trivial SPT by the SPT entangler, we obtain the Hamiltonian of the SPT: \begin{equation}H = - \sum_{v} \adjincludegraphics[width=3cm,valign=c]{entangler_1.pdf}, \label{eq:type-ISPT}\end{equation} After applying the gauging map [Section~\ref{sec:gauging_map ], we obtain stabilizers of 3d toric code in the bulk.}
elements.sent-2411-859: However, on the domain wall, we obtain the Hamiltonian of the type-I $\mathbb{Z _2$ twisted quantum double, which is similar to the Hamiltonian of the quantum double model but includes an extra phase factor $W^g_v$ associated with each vertex~\cite{hu2013twisted,li2023symmetry .}}

elements.sent-2411-861: To that end, recall that the gauging maps the vertex degrees of freedom to the edge degrees of freedom [Section~\ref{sec:gauging_map ].}

elements.sent-2411-864: Applying this relation to every $\mathrm{CZ}$ gate in Eq.~\eqref{eq:type-ISPT , we obtain \begin{equation}\adjincludegraphics[width=0.3\linewidth,valign=c]{phaseTQD.pdf},\label{eq:phaseTQD}\end{equation} where the red wavy lines represent $\mathrm{CZ}$ gates between the connected two qubits.}
elements.sent-2411-865: While Eq.~\eqref{eq:Z2TQD is not known in the literature in its exact form, we note that it can be related to a known exactly solvable model of double semions.}
elements.sent-2411-866: More specifically, if we remove the edges perpendicular to the triangular lattice, its ground state becomes exactly the ground state of the model in Ref.~\cite{ellison2022pauli .}
elements.sent-2411-867: To see this, note the following relation between a $\mathrm{CZ}$ gate and a product of $S$ and $S^{\dagger}$ gates on a flux-free triangle: \begin{equation}\adjincludegraphics[width=0.3\linewidth,valign=c]{CZS.pdf}.\label{eq:CZS}\end{equation} Using these relations, we can change the star operator as follows: \begin{equation}\adjincludegraphics[width=0.65\linewidth,valign=c]{phaseTQD_2.pdf}.\label{eq:phaseTQD_2}\end{equation} Here we conjugated each site on the triangular lattice by $S$ and $S^{\dagger}$ to remove Pauli $Z$ operators.
